fact: February 5 is exactly 36 days into the year, which means you've already lived through 10% of the entire year by this date!
In the Northern Hemisphere, groundhogs are typically still fast asleep underground on this date, dreaming through their winter hibernation with their heart rate slowed to just 5 beats per minute.
The air humidity often drops to its lowest levels of the entire year in many cold regions during early February, which is why your fingers might feel extra dry and crackly around February 5.
February 5 falls in the middle of Aquarius season, making it one of the most 'watery' days of the year according to astrology, even though it's freezing cold outside in many places.
Maple trees in cold climates are secretly making sugar around February 5! The freeze-thaw cycles create perfect pressure for sap to start flowing, even though the trees look completely dead.
In Australia and other Southern Hemisphere countries, February 5 hits right in the peak of summer heat, with temperatures that can melt chocolate bars in your backpack.
The sun typically rises about 2 minutes earlier each day around February 5 in northern regions, which means you gain 14 whole minutes of daylight during this single week.
February 5 sits exactly 46 days before the spring equinox, making it the mathematical middle of late winter in the Northern Hemisphere.
Polar bears in the Arctic are often giving birth to tiny, hairless cubs around this date - the babies are only the size of hamsters and can't even see yet!

Snow crystals typically form in completely different shapes around February 5 compared to December because the air temperature and humidity create perfect conditions for those classic six-pointed stars.
In many northern cities, February 5 often marks the deepest frost penetration into the ground - the frozen layer underground reaches its maximum depth of the entire winter.
The moon usually appears extra bright and crisp around early February dates like the 5th because the cold, dry winter air has less moisture to blur the moonlight.
